topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the flat, impersonal efficiency of a system update. Dr. Albright was departing. I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the subject line. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed too large for the quiet apartment. I didn't feel a spike of panic, nor a surge of excitement. Just a dull settling in my chest, like dust finally finding its resting place. I picked up my lukewarm mug of tea, the ceramic warm against my palms, and took a deliberate, slow sip. The weight of the impending change felt less like a crisis and more like a minor inconvenience—a slightly altered trajectory on a road I was already planning to take. I turned back to my laptop, the words on the screen suddenly seeming less urgent than the pattern of the condensation tracing paths down the glass of the windowpane.

[story 2]

"So, he's leaving?" I mumbled, not bothering to look up from the textbook. The words tasted like ash in my mouth. My shoulders slumped further into the worn fabric of my hoodie. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the slight roughness of the strands beneath my fingers. There was no sharp pang of loss, just a vast, empty space where anticipation used to reside. I shifted in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and watched a fly buzz lazily near the lamp. It was just a momentary distraction, a flicker of movement in an otherwise static room. I closed the book, placing it on the desk with a soft thud. The reality of the situation registered as simply another data point, something to be filed away and moved on from.

[story 3]

I reread the advisory memo for the third time. The language was clinical, devoid of the usual warmth Dr. Albright always injected into departmental correspondence. My jaw remained slack as I processed the information. I stood up, stretching languidly, the movement feeling practiced rather than motivated. The air in the office felt strangely thin. I walked to the window, looking out at the campus quad where students bustled with purpose, their conversations sharp and engaged. I didn't feel the need to confront the news, nor did I feel the urge to plan a sudden rescue strategy. It was just a fact now, an external event that required minimal internal processing. I turned away from the view, the decision made internally to simply resume my routine, unperturbed.
